title: Rabbit pregnancy and kindling preparation
subtitle: How to confirm a doe is in kindle, what to do in the week before the litter is due, and what to check in the first 24 hours.
excerpt: Rabbit gestation is 31 days and the doe gives almost no external signs of pregnancy in the first three weeks. Preparation for kindling starts at day 25: providing a nest box, ensuring privacy and a suitable temperature, and not disturbing the nest after kitting. The first 24 hours after birth are critical for kit survival.
sourceNotes: RSPCA: rabbit breeding and pregnancy care. British Rabbit Council: breeding standards and kindling management. Varga (2013), Textbook of Rabbit Medicine (2nd ed.): reproduction chapter.

glossaryTerms.definition: The process of a doe giving birth to a litter of rabbits. Most does kindle in the early hours of the morning, often before the keeper arrives. Kindling is usually rapid; does often deliver a full litter of 4 to 12 kits within 30 minutes.

The behaviour in which a pregnant doe plucks fur from her own dewlap and belly in the days immediately before kindling, and lines the nest box with the fur. A sign that kindling is imminent. Does that do not pull fur voluntarily may need supplementary bedding material placed in the nest box.

Hypothermia in newborn rabbit kits, which are born hairless and cannot thermoregulate. The most common cause of kit death in the first 24 hours. Kits found scattered outside the nest box, or in a nest that has insufficient fur covering, should be gathered back into the nest immediately. A chilled but still-living kit may revive if warmed gradually.

content.text: A successfully mated doe will kindle 31 days after mating. With that date known, preparation starts at day 25 to ensure everything is in place before the litter arrives. Most does that have been well prepared and provided with a suitable nest box will kindle without any intervention from the keeper.

At day 10 to 14, a vet or experienced breeder can palpate the abdomen gently to feel the developing embryos as marble-sized spheres in the uterus. This requires practice and a quiet, compliant doe. A simpler indicator is the doe's response to re-mating: at day 10 after first mating, offer the doe to the buck again. A pregnant doe will typically reject the buck vigorously; a non-pregnant doe will allow mating. This is not 100% reliable but is a practical early indicator.

At day 25 to 27, place the nest box in the doe's housing. The nest box should be large enough for the doe to enter and turn around, with a lip at the entrance high enough to retain kits but low enough for the doe to step over comfortably. Line the box with clean straw or hay. Watch for
: a doe plucking her own fur and depositing it in the nest is within 48 hours of

Check the nest box quietly within two hours of the expected kindling and again at 12 hours. Count the kits. Remove any dead kits. Check the nest fur coverage: kits should be buried under a layer of the doe's fur. If the nest is thin, add extra hay and gently move the kits to the centre.
in the first 12 hours is the most common cause of kit mortality; a scattered litter outside the nest box on a cold night will not survive unaided.

Does nurse briefly once or twice a day, usually at night. A doe that does not appear to have nursed for 24 hours, kits that are wrinkled and thin-skinned with no visible milk belly, or a doe that is sitting in the nest box continuously rather than coming out to eat, all warrant investigation. Contact your vet if you are unsure whether kits are being fed.
